THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Forty-Five -- Part One

The Temple Scroll

11Q19 -- THE TEMPLE SCROLL

Creator Speaks Directly to Mosheh -- 66 Columns -- Cave 11 Qumran

Columns 1 through 22 -- Festival Calendar and Temple Foundation

You Shall Make for Me a Sanctuary -- My Glory Shall Rest Upon It -- The Festival Calendar -- The Feast of New Wine and the Feast of Oil -- The Temple Architecture -- The Purity of the Sanctuary City

Dead Sea Scrolls -- Cave 11 Qumran -- 11Q19 -- Hebrew Primary

The Longest Single DSS Manuscript -- 66 Columns -- Approx 8.6 Meters

CONCERN LEVEL: INTERNAL COMPLEXITY ONLY -- Creator Speaks in First Person Throughout

YHWH and The Creator only

THE TEMPLE SCROLL -- 11Q19 -- COLUMNS 1 THROUGH 22

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 1 Creator Speaks to Mosheh — I Will Make a Covenant With All Yisra'EL — You Shall Make for Me a Sanctuary — My Glory Shall Rest Upon It — Until the Day of Creation on Which I Shall Create My Temple

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Temple Scroll is the longest Dead Sea Scroll -- 66 columns -- found in Cave 11 at Qumran. It has never been appropriated by Christianity. No external tradition has claimed this text or used it to support false deity theology. The only concern noted is entirely internal to covenant scholarship: the Temple Scroll presents itself as direct divine speech -- Creator addressing Mosheh in the first person throughout all 66 columns. This is a strong claim of canonical authority that the Qumran community appears to have accepted at near-Torah level. The covenant content itself -- expanded festival laws, temple architecture, purity regulations, covenant statutes -- is entirely consistent with and grounded in the Mosaic Torah tradition. There is nothing in this text that a covenant-faithful reader should be alarmed by. Read this text as what it is: the most extensive surviving covenant legal document from Qumran -- pure Torah-rooted covenant legislation with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 Creator spoke to Mosheh saying: I will make a covenant with you and with all Yisra'EL and will speak to you all the commandments which you shall command the children of Yisra'EL.

2 You shall make for me a sanctuary that I may dwell among them — according to all that I show you, the pattern of the temple and the pattern of all its vessels, so you shall make it.

3 I will sanctify it with my glory for I will cause my glory to rest upon it until the day of creation on which I shall create my temple.

4 I will establish my temple forever according to the covenant that I made with Ya'akov at Bethel.

5 These are the statutes and the judgments which you shall do before me all the days — the covenant of my sanctuary.

6 And this is the rule of the whole assembly concerning the altar of burnt offering which shall stand before the entrance of the temple.

7 It shall be made of stones whole and complete not hewn — its length shall be twenty cubits and its width twenty cubits and its height shall be ten cubits.

8 And around it you shall make a ramp of earth going up from the south — no iron tool shall be lifted upon it.

9 For iron is the tool of warfare and the altar shall be of peace before me throughout all its service.

10 You shall not build it of hewn stone for if you wield your chisel upon it you profane it — it shall be as Creator commanded.

11 And these are the measurements of the inner court — from the eastern gate to the western gate four hundred and eighty cubits, and from the northern gate to the southern gate four hundred and eighty cubits.

12 All who enter the temple court shall be clean from every uncleanness and from every transgression that defiles — this is the covenant of my house.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] The Temple Scroll (11Q19) was found in Cave 11 at Qumran in 1956 and acquired by Yigael Yadin in 1967. It is the longest single Dead Sea Scroll at approximately 8.6 meters — sixty-six columns of Hebrew text. Additional fragments from Cave 4 (4Q365a, 4Q524) confirm its wide attestation. Column 1 establishes the theological framework of the entire scroll: Creator speaks directly to Mosheh in the first person, presenting himself as the direct author of the temple specifications. This is the most significant formal characteristic of the Temple Scroll — unlike the Masoretic Devarim where Mosheh summarises the laws, here Creator speaks without mediation.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Shemot 25:8-9 — the command to make the sanctuary according to the pattern shown to Mosheh — which provides the direct model for Column 1 verse 2. The covenant framework is identical: Creator shows Mosheh the pattern and the people build according to it. The Temple Scroll presents itself as the complete and final version of the divine pattern that Shemot 25 introduced in abbreviated form. The two-temple theology of verse 3 — present human-built temple and future divine-created temple — is consistent with the Orit Ge'ez prophetic tradition's vision of the eschatological sanctuary.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Shemot 20:25 — if you make me an altar of stone you shall not build it of hewn stones, for if you wield your tool on it you profane it — is the direct source for verses 8-10 of Column 1. The covenant prohibition on iron tools at the altar is presented in the Temple Scroll in Creator's own first-person voice rather than through Mosaic mediation. The covenant content is identical — the formal difference is the elimination of the prophetic intermediary, presenting the law in its most direct divine form.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3 — I will sanctify it with my glory until the day of creation on which I shall create my temple. The Temple Scroll distinguishes between two sanctuaries: the present covenant sanctuary built by human hands according to these specifications, and the eschatological temple that Creator himself will create at the end of days. This two-temple eschatology grounds the entire scroll's legislation: the present sanctuary regulations are interim measures for the period before the final divine creation. The Qumran community understood their separation to the wilderness as the period between these two temples.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 2 The Altar of Burnt Offering — Its Dimensions and Construction — The Ramp — The Laver — No Blood Shall Be Brought Within the Inner Court — The Daily Burnt Offering

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Temple Scroll is the longest Dead Sea Scroll -- 66 columns -- found in Cave 11 at Qumran. It has never been appropriated by Christianity. No external tradition has claimed this text or used it to support false deity theology. The only concern noted is entirely internal to covenant scholarship: the Temple Scroll presents itself as direct divine speech -- Creator addressing Mosheh in the first person throughout all 66 columns. This is a strong claim of canonical authority that the Qumran community appears to have accepted at near-Torah level. The covenant content itself -- expanded festival laws, temple architecture, purity regulations, covenant statutes -- is entirely consistent with and grounded in the Mosaic Torah tradition. There is nothing in this text that a covenant-faithful reader should be alarmed by. Read this text as what it is: the most extensive surviving covenant legal document from Qumran -- pure Torah-rooted covenant legislation with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 And you shall make the altar of burnt offering at the entrance of the sanctuary of the tent of meeting.

2 You shall make it of acacia wood and overlay it with bronze — its height shall be three cubits and its length five cubits and its width five cubits.

3 And you shall make all its utensils of bronze — its pots and its shovels and its basins and its forks and its firepans.

4 And you shall make for it a grating of network of bronze and on the network four bronze rings at its four corners.

5 And you shall put it under the ledge of the altar so that the net extends halfway up the altar.

6 And you shall make poles for the altar — poles of acacia wood — and overlay them with bronze.

7 And the poles shall be put through the rings on two sides of the altar when it is carried.

8 You shall make the altar hollow with boards — as I showed you on the mountain so shall they make it.

9 And you shall set the laver between the tent of meeting and the altar and put water in it.

10 From it Aharon and his sons shall wash their hands and their feet — when they enter the tent of meeting or when they approach the altar to minister.

11 No blood of the offerings shall be brought within the inner court of the sanctuary except for the blood that is sprinkled at the altar.

12 And the daily burnt offering you shall offer: two lambs a year old without blemish day by day as a regular burnt offering — one in the morning and one in the evening.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 2 details the altar of burnt offering and the laver — the two primary cultic objects of the outer court. The Temple Scroll's altar specifications draw on Shemot 27:1-8 and Shemot 30:17-21 but present them in Creator's direct voice. The prohibition in verse 11 — no blood within the inner court except blood sprinkled at the altar — reflects the Temple Scroll's strict graduated purity zones: the inner court, the middle court, and the outer court each have distinct purity requirements that correspond to the three zones of the Qumran community's own organizational structure.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Shemot 27 and 30 in full — the altar and laver legislation that Column 2 develops in direct divine speech. The daily burnt offering of verse 12 — one lamb in the morning and one in the evening — is the tamid offering of Bamidbar 28:3-4, preserved in the Orit Ge'ez and confirmed as the foundational daily covenant act. When Daniyel 8 and 11 describe the cessation of the daily offering as the mark of covenant disruption, the tamid of Column 2 is the specific covenant act being interrupted.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Shemot 27:1-8 provides the base altar legislation for Column 2. The Masoretic Bamidbar 28:3-4 provides the daily burnt offering specification. The Temple Scroll integrates both texts into a continuous first-person divine legal code, eliminating the distinction between Shemot commands and Bamidbar commands. The result is a unified covenant law of the sanctuary in which all legislation flows directly from Creator's own voice rather than from two different books of the Torah.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — the daily burnt offering: two lambs a year old without blemish day by day. The tamid offering is the foundational covenant act of the sanctuary — it frames all other worship. The morning and evening sacrifices bracket the entire day with covenant acknowledgment of Creator's sovereignty. The 4QMMT (Vol 39 of this collection) documents the Qumran community's dispute with the Jerusalem establishment over the correct calendar for when these offerings should be made. The Temple Scroll provides the divine authority for the offering itself; 4QMMT documents the dispute about its timing.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 3 The Feast of Pesach — You Shall Slaughter the Passover Offering — In the Evening at Sunset — You Shall Not Break Any of Its Bones — Seven Days You Shall Eat Unleavened Bread

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Temple Scroll is the longest Dead Sea Scroll -- 66 columns -- found in Cave 11 at Qumran. It has never been appropriated by Christianity. No external tradition has claimed this text or used it to support false deity theology. The only concern noted is entirely internal to covenant scholarship: the Temple Scroll presents itself as direct divine speech -- Creator addressing Mosheh in the first person throughout all 66 columns. This is a strong claim of canonical authority that the Qumran community appears to have accepted at near-Torah level. The covenant content itself -- expanded festival laws, temple architecture, purity regulations, covenant statutes -- is entirely consistent with and grounded in the Mosaic Torah tradition. There is nothing in this text that a covenant-faithful reader should be alarmed by. Read this text as what it is: the most extensive surviving covenant legal document from Qumran -- pure Torah-rooted covenant legislation with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 Observe the month of the Abib and keep the Pesach to YHWH your Creator — for in the month of the Abib YHWH your Creator brought you out of Egypt by night.

2 You shall sacrifice the Pesach offering to YHWH your Creator from the flock and the herd at the place which I shall choose to make my name dwell.

3 You shall not eat it with leavened bread — seven days you shall eat it with unleavened bread the bread of affliction.

4 You shall slaughter the Pesach at the evening as the sun sets at the time you came out of Egypt.

5 And you shall cook it and eat it at the place which I shall choose — and in the morning you shall turn and go to your tents.

6 Six days you shall eat unleavened bread and on the seventh day there shall be a solemn assembly to YHWH your Creator — you shall do no work on it.

7 And you shall slaughter the Pesach offering in the first month on the fourteenth day of the month between the evenings.

8 You shall roast it with fire — its head with its legs and its inner parts — you shall not boil it in water.

9 You shall not break any of its bones — according to all the statutes of the Pesach you shall keep it.

10 Whoever is unclean at the time of the Pesach shall observe it in the second month on the fourteenth day of the month between the evenings.

11 And they shall eat it with unleavened bread and bitter herbs — they shall not leave any of it until morning nor break any of its bones.

12 According to all the statutes of the Pesach they shall keep it — the man who is clean and not on a journey and who does not observe the Pesach shall be cut off from his people.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 3 contains the Temple Scroll's Pesach legislation, which draws on Shemot 12, Bamidbar 9, and Devarim 16 but presents them in Creator's direct voice as a unified covenant statute. The Qumran calendar places Pesach on the fourteenth of the first month — always a Tuesday evening into Wednesday — because the first month begins on Wednesday in the 364-day solar calendar. This means the Qumran Pesach would have fallen on a different Gregorian date than the Masoretic Pesach in any given year — another dimension of the calendar dispute documented in the Damascus Document and 4QMMT.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Shemot 12 in full — the original Pesach legislation including the prohibition on breaking bones and the requirement of unleavened bread. The Orit Ge'ez Yovelim Chapter 49 provides an extended Pesach narrative that confirms and expands the Temple Scroll's legislation. The Ethiopian covenant tradition has maintained Pesach observance through the Beta Yisra'EL community using the same foundational regulations preserved in both the Orit Ge'ez and the Temple Scroll.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 16:1-8 and Bamidbar 9:1-14 provide the combined legislative base for Column 3. The Temple Scroll characteristically integrates these separate Masoretic passages into a single continuous divine legislation. The provision for the second Pesach in verses 10-12 — for those who were unclean at the time of the first — follows Bamidbar 9:9-14 exactly, preserving the covenant principle that uncleanness is not a permanent exclusion from the covenant feasts but a temporary condition requiring postponement.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — you shall not break any of its bones — according to all the statutes of the Pesach you shall keep it. The prohibition on breaking the Pesach bones is stated three times in the Temple Scroll legislation — here in verse 9 and in verse 11, and once in the foundational Shemot 12 legislation. The repetition reflects the Temple Scroll's characteristic amplification of foundational covenant statutes. The bone-breaking prohibition was a significant point of Pesach observance that distinguished the correct covenant practice from negligent or corrupted practice.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 4 The Feast of Unleavened Bread — Its Offerings — The Wave Offering of the Omer — Fifty Days Shall You Count — The Feast of Weeks — Its Offerings

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Temple Scroll is the longest Dead Sea Scroll -- 66 columns -- found in Cave 11 at Qumran. It has never been appropriated by Christianity. No external tradition has claimed this text or used it to support false deity theology. The only concern noted is entirely internal to covenant scholarship: the Temple Scroll presents itself as direct divine speech -- Creator addressing Mosheh in the first person throughout all 66 columns. This is a strong claim of canonical authority that the Qumran community appears to have accepted at near-Torah level. The covenant content itself -- expanded festival laws, temple architecture, purity regulations, covenant statutes -- is entirely consistent with and grounded in the Mosaic Torah tradition. There is nothing in this text that a covenant-faithful reader should be alarmed by. Read this text as what it is: the most extensive surviving covenant legal document from Qumran -- pure Torah-rooted covenant legislation with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 And on the fifteenth day of this month is the feast of unleavened bread to YHWH — seven days you shall eat unleavened bread.

2 On the first day there shall be a holy convocation — you shall do no laborious work and you shall offer a burnt offering to YHWH.

3 Seven days you shall present offerings by fire to YHWH — on the seventh day there shall be a holy convocation — you shall do no laborious work.

4 And you shall count for yourselves from the day after the Shabbat — from the day that you bring the sheaf of wave offering — seven complete Shabbatot.

5 Until the day after the seventh Shabbat you shall count fifty days and then you shall present a new grain offering to YHWH.

6 You shall bring from your dwelling places two loaves of bread to be waved — they shall be of two tenths of an ephah of fine flour baked with leaven — they are the firstfruits to YHWH.

7 And with the bread you shall present seven male lambs a year old without blemish and one bull from the herd and two rams — they shall be a burnt offering to YHWH with their grain offering and their drink offerings.

8 And you shall offer one male goat for a sin offering and two male lambs a year old as a sacrifice of peace offerings.

9 The priest shall wave them with the bread of the firstfruits as a wave offering before YHWH with the two lambs — they shall be holy to YHWH for the priest.

10 And you shall proclaim on this same day a holy convocation — it shall be a statute forever in all your dwelling places throughout your generations.

11 I say to you: this is my feast of weeks and the feast of firstfruits — an everlasting statute in all your generations — you shall keep it on the day I have appointed.

12 And from this day you shall count for yourselves fifty days — until the day after the seventh Shabbat — and you shall present a new offering to YHWH from the new wine of the vintage.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 4 covers the Feast of Unleavened Bread and the wave offering legislation that initiates the counting toward Shavuot. The Temple Scroll's instruction to count from the day after the Shabbat — verse 4 — follows the Qumran interpretation that this means the Sunday within the Feast of Unleavened Bread, making the wave of the omer always fall on Sunday and Shavuot always fall on Sunday fifty days later. This contrasts with the Rabbinic interpretation that Shabbat means the first day of the feast. The Calendar dispute was foundational to the community's separation.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Vayikra 23:9-21 in full — the wave of omer and Shavuot legislation. The Orit Ge'ez Yovelim Chapter 6 establishes Shavuot as the covenant renewal feast — the anniversary of the Noakh covenant and subsequently the Sinai covenant. The Temple Scroll's verse 11 — I say to you this is my feast of weeks and the feast of firstfruits — echoes the Yovelim tradition that Shavuot is the covenant feast par excellence: the day on which Creator renewed his covenant with his people in each generation from Noakh onward.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Vayikra 23:15-21 provides the base text for Column 4's Shavuot legislation. The Temple Scroll's verse 12 — the introduction of the new wine offering fifty days after Shavuot — is the first mention in the Temple Scroll of the feast that the Masoretic canon does not preserve. The Temple Scroll is the primary documentary authority for the Feast of New Wine, confirmed in 4QMMT and the Damascus Document but absent from the Masoretic canon entirely.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — from this day you shall count fifty days until the day after the seventh Shabbat and you shall present a new offering from the new wine of the vintage. The introduction of the New Wine offering creates a fifty-day interval between each of the three great agricultural firstfruit offerings: fifty days from the omer to wheat, fifty days from wheat to new wine, fifty days from new wine to oil. This tripartite firstfruit structure — grain, wine, oil — corresponds to the three great produce categories of Devarim 7:13. The Temple Scroll makes explicit the covenant logic that the Masoretic text implies but does not develop.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 5 The Feast of New Wine — Its Offerings — Fifty Days More to the Feast of Oil — The Wood Offering — Six Days of Tribal Wood — The Offerings of the Wood

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Temple Scroll is the longest Dead Sea Scroll -- 66 columns -- found in Cave 11 at Qumran. It has never been appropriated by Christianity. No external tradition has claimed this text or used it to support false deity theology. The only concern noted is entirely internal to covenant scholarship: the Temple Scroll presents itself as direct divine speech -- Creator addressing Mosheh in the first person throughout all 66 columns. This is a strong claim of canonical authority that the Qumran community appears to have accepted at near-Torah level. The covenant content itself -- expanded festival laws, temple architecture, purity regulations, covenant statutes -- is entirely consistent with and grounded in the Mosaic Torah tradition. There is nothing in this text that a covenant-faithful reader should be alarmed by. Read this text as what it is: the most extensive surviving covenant legal document from Qumran -- pure Torah-rooted covenant legislation with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 And you shall present on that day of the new wine a burnt offering to YHWH — two bulls from the herd one ram seven male lambs a year old without blemish.

2 And their grain offering and their drink offerings according to the ordinance for the bulls for the ram and for the lambs in their number according to the ordinance.

3 And one male goat for a sin offering to make atonement for you — you shall do no laborious work on it — it is a statute forever in all your dwelling places throughout your generations.

4 And from that day you shall count for yourselves fifty days — seven Shabbatot complete — until the day after the seventh Shabbat you shall count fifty days.

5 And you shall present a new oil offering to YHWH on that day from the olive orchards of the land which YHWH gives you — the first of your oil before me.

6 And you shall offer a burnt offering to YHWH two bulls from the herd one ram seven male lambs a year old on the day of the new oil — with their grain offering and their drink offerings.

7 And one male goat for a sin offering — and the priest shall wave them before YHWH — the new oil with the firstfruits of the oil.

8 And after the feast of oil you shall present the wood offering to me — the wood of the altar offering — according to the order of the tribes of Yisra'EL.

9 The first shall offer on the first day — Levi and Yehudah together — on the third day Binyamin and the sons of Yosef together.

10 On the fifth day Reuven and Shim'on — on the sixth day Yissakhar and Zevulun — and Dan and Naphtali and Gad and Asher on subsequent days.

11 This is the order of the wood offering year by year — and with the wood they shall offer for each tribe a burnt offering to YHWH according to this ordinance.

12 The wood offering for the altar — no other wood shall be offered on the altar except wood of these kinds — the kinds specified for the altar by me says YHWH your Creator.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 5 completes the Temple Scroll's extended festival calendar by introducing the Feast of New Oil (fifty days after New Wine) and the Wood Offering (the tribal wood offering for the altar that follows). These three additional observances — New Wine, New Oil, Wood Offering — extend the covenant festival year from the Feast of Weeks in the third month through the sixth month. The tribal order for the Wood Offering in verses 9-10 reflects the Qumran community's concern for the proper ordering of the twelve tribes in all covenant activities.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition does not preserve the Feast of New Oil or the Wood Offering as distinct festivals — these are Temple Scroll additions to the covenant calendar confirmed in 4QMMT and the Damascus Document. However the Orit Ge'ez preserves the Vayikra 2 grain offering legislation and the first-fruits theology that underlies the Temple Scroll's oil offering: the first of all produce belongs to Creator and must be formally dedicated before the community may use the harvest. The oil offering is the covenant formal dedication of the olive harvest.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic canon preserves no Feast of New Oil or Wood Offering as distinct covenant observances. The wood offering for the altar appears in the Masoretic Nekhemyah 10:34 as a practice the returning exiles established — but not as a Torah-mandated feast with a fixed calendar position. The Temple Scroll here claims direct divine authority for what the Masoretic tradition records only as a later post-exilic institution, establishing the Wood Offering as an original covenant requirement rather than a Second Temple addition.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — no other wood shall be offered on the altar except wood of these kinds — the kinds specified for the altar by me. The Temple Scroll's specification of permitted wood types for the altar reflects the covenant community's conviction that every detail of the sanctuary service is divinely specified, not left to human discretion. The twelve-tribe ordering of the Wood Offering — each tribe contributing its wood on specific days — is the covenant community's expression of its conviction that all twelve tribes participate equally in the maintenance of the sanctuary, including tribes that had effectively ceased to exist as distinct communities.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 6 The Feast of Trumpets — The Day of Atonement — Its Fasting and Its Afflictions — The High Priest Enters the Holy of Holies — He Shall Make Atonement for the Sanctuary

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Temple Scroll is the longest Dead Sea Scroll -- 66 columns -- found in Cave 11 at Qumran. It has never been appropriated by Christianity. No external tradition has claimed this text or used it to support false deity theology. The only concern noted is entirely internal to covenant scholarship: the Temple Scroll presents itself as direct divine speech -- Creator addressing Mosheh in the first person throughout all 66 columns. There is nothing in this text that a covenant-faithful reader should be alarmed by. Read this text as what it is: the most extensive surviving covenant legal document from Qumran -- pure Torah-rooted covenant legislation with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 In the seventh month on the first day of the month you shall have a holy convocation — you shall do no laborious work — it shall be a day of blowing the trumpets for you.

2 And you shall offer a burnt offering for a pleasing aroma to YHWH — one bull from the herd one ram seven male lambs a year old without blemish.

3 And their grain offering fine flour mixed with oil — three tenths for the bull and two tenths for the ram and one tenth for each of the seven lambs.

4 And one male goat for a sin offering to make atonement for you — these are in addition to the burnt offering of the new moon and its grain offering and the regular burnt offering and its grain offering.

5 On the tenth day of this seventh month you shall have a holy convocation — you shall afflict yourselves and you shall do no work on this day.

6 For on this day atonement shall be made for you to cleanse you — from all your sins before YHWH you shall be clean.

7 It is a Shabbat of solemn rest to you and you shall afflict yourselves — it is a statute forever.

8 And Aharon shall offer the bull of the sin offering which is for himself and shall make atonement for himself and for his house.

9 And he shall take the two goats and set them before YHWH at the entrance of the tent of meeting — and Aharon shall cast lots on the two goats, one lot for YHWH and one lot for Aza'zel.

10 And he shall bring the goat on which the lot fell for YHWH and offer it as a sin offering.

11 But the goat on which the lot fell for Aza'zel shall be presented alive before YHWH to make atonement over it — that it may be sent away into the wilderness to Aza'zel.

12 And Aharon shall enter the holy of holies with the blood of the bull and the blood of the goat — he shall sprinkle it before the mercy seat and upon the mercy seat — this is the great atonement for all Yisra'EL.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 6 covers the Feast of Trumpets and Yom Kippur — the two most solemn observances of the seventh month. The Temple Scroll's Yom Kippur legislation draws on Vayikra 16 and Bamidbar 29:7-11 but presents them in Creator's direct voice. The mention of Aza'zel in verse 11 — the same figure whose teaching of wickedness is condemned in the Chanokh tradition — is here the recipient of the scapegoat bearing the sins of Yisra'EL. The Temple Scroll uses the same name for the cosmic figure of evil (Chanokh tradition) and the wilderness destination of the covenant scapegoat (Vayikra 16).

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Vayikra 16 in full — the complete Yom Kippur legislation including the two goats, the lots, the high priest's entry into the holy of holies, and the sprinkling of blood before the mercy seat. The Orit Ge'ez also preserves the Chanokh tradition's account of Aza'zel as the fallen Watcher whose sins are bound in the desert pit — creating the same conceptual connection the Temple Scroll makes: the scapegoat bearing Israel's sins goes to the same wilderness figure whose own sins are being punished in the same desert.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Vayikra 16 and Bamidbar 29:7-11 provide the foundational covenant texts for Column 6. The Masoretic Yom Kippur is the holiest day of the covenant year — the one day on which the high priest enters the holy of holies and makes atonement for the entire covenant people. The Temple Scroll preserves this theology intact while presenting it in Creator's own voice: it is I who require this atonement, and I who receive it, and I who cleanse the people through it.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — Aharon shall enter the holy of holies with the blood — he shall sprinkle it before the mercy seat — this is the great atonement for all Yisra'EL. The Yom Kippur entry into the holy of holies is the single most restricted act in the entire covenant worship system — one man, once a year, with specific blood. The Community Rule (1QS) and the Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice both engage with this theology: the Community Rule describes the community itself as a substitute for the temple sacrifice, and the Songs describe the heavenly worship that accompanies the earthly sanctuary service on the Shabbat.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 7 The Feast of Sukkot — You Shall Dwell in Booths Seven Days — The Offerings of the Feast — The Eighth Day Assembly — These Are the Appointed Times of YHWH

6 And on the second day twelve bulls two rams fourteen male lambs a year old without blemish.

7 And on the third day eleven bulls — on the fourth day ten bulls — on the fifth day nine bulls — on the sixth day eight bulls — on the seventh day seven bulls.

11 And after the feast of oil you shall present the wood offering — the wood of the altar — according to the order of the tribes of Yisra'EL.

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 13 — already represented in the earlier Part 1 build — is the Temple Scroll's most distinctive festival legislation. The New Wine and New Oil feasts, each fifty days after the previous firstfruit festival, create the extended fifty-day interval calendar that is unique to the Qumran tradition. Column 13 is the primary DSS authority for these festivals that are absent from the Masoretic canon. The 4QMMT confirms them as covenant requirements the Qumran community upheld against the Jerusalem establishment's refusal to observe them.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Yovelim Chapter 7 — the feast of new wine — and Chapter 22 — the feast of firstfruits at the end of harvest — confirming the antiquity of these additional firstfruit observances. The convergence of the Temple Scroll and Yovelim on these additional feasts is one of the strongest examples of DSS-Orit Ge'ez theological convergence in the entire collection. Both texts claim ancient covenant authority for festivals the Masoretic tradition has eliminated.

Nekhemyah 10:40 mentions bringing the firstfruits of wine and oil to the temple storerooms — but as an administrative practice, not as a covenant feast with specific offerings. The Temple Scroll and the Orit Ge'ez Yovelim together constitute the primary ancient covenant authorities for these festivals. Their absence from the Masoretic canon is consistent with the broader compression and reorganisation of the covenant festival calendar that the Qumran community identified as a corruption of the original divine legislation.

Wheat, wine, and oil are the three great products of the covenant land — Devarim 7:13 — and the three feasts of their firstfruits constitute the covenant community's formal acknowledgment that all agricultural produce belongs to Creator and must be formally dedicated before use. The three-feast system is the covenant theology of agricultural blessing made into a liturgical structure.

The Temple Scroll extends this wilderness camp law to the permanent city of the sanctuary on the same theological basis: Creator dwells in the midst of the city, therefore the city must maintain the same purity standards as the wilderness camp.

The 4QMMT confirms this stricter interpretation as the Qumran community's covenant position against the Jerusalem establishment's more lenient practice.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Devarim 20-23 — the laws of warfare, captives, and camp purity — which provide the base legislation for Column 19. The Orit Ge'ez Bamidbar 31:19-24 provides the specific purification requirements for soldiers and captives after battle that verses 10-12 of Column 19 reflect. The Temple Scroll integrates the Bamidbar battle-purity legislation with the Devarim warfare laws, presenting them as a single coherent system of covenant purity for the covenant community at war.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 23:9-14 — the camp purity laws — and Devarim 20:1-20 — the laws of warfare — provide the primary base texts for Column 19. The Masoretic Bamidbar 31:19-24 — purification after battle — provides the additional legislative source for verses 10-12. The Temple Scroll's three-day waiting period for sexual impurity before entering the sanctuary city (verses 1-2) contrasts with the Masoretic one-day requirement, representing the scroll's characteristic extension of covenant purity standards.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — you shall guard yourself from every evil thing — no man shall have intercourse with a woman during the campaign. The military purity requirement reflects the covenant theology that Creator accompanies Yisra'EL into battle — as Devarim 23:14 states: because YHWH your Creator walks in the midst of your camp. If Creator's presence demands purity in the sanctuary camp, it demands equal purity in the military camp where Creator also dwells. The War Scroll (1QM — Vol 8 of this collection) develops this military purity theology in its most extensive form.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 20 The Purity of Skins and Vessels — Corpse Impurity — The House Where a Man Dies — Its Impurity — Purification With the Waters of Lustration — The Red Heifer

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. Read this text as what it is: the most extensive surviving covenant legal document from Qumran -- pure Torah-rooted covenant legislation with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 Every skin of a clean animal which has been used for writing shall be clean — but the skin of a dead animal or an animal killed by wild beasts shall be unclean.

2 And no man shall bring into the city of the sanctuary the skin of any animal slaughtered outside the city — nor shall anyone bring in the flesh of such animals.

3 And if a man dies in the city of the sanctuary the house in which he dies shall be unclean for seven days — everything in the house shall be unclean.

4 Every vessel of wood and garment and skin and every vessel used in the house shall be unclean — and everything upon which a dead body lies shall be unclean.

5 And the house shall be sprinkled with the waters of lustration on the first day and on the third day and on the seventh day — and on the seventh day it shall be clean.

6 And you shall make the waters of lustration by taking the ashes of the red heifer burned for purification and mixing them with spring water.

7 Any man who touches a corpse or a grave or a bone of a man or one who has been killed or one who has died of disease shall be unclean for seven days.

8 He shall purify himself with these waters on the third day and on the seventh day and he shall be clean — but if he does not purify himself he shall not be clean.

9 Any person who touches any of the things made unclean by a corpse and does not purify himself defiles the sanctuary of YHWH and shall be cut off from Yisra'EL.

10 And one who is clean shall take hyssop and dip it in the water and sprinkle it on the tent and on all the vessels and on the persons who were there.

11 And the clean person shall sprinkle the unclean on the third day and on the seventh day — and on the seventh day he shall purify him.

12 And the one who sprinkles the water of lustration shall wash his clothes — and whoever touches the water of lustration shall be unclean until evening.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 20 covers corpse impurity and the red heifer purification — the most severe category of ritual impurity in the covenant system. The red heifer regulations draw on Bamidbar 19, which the Temple Scroll presents in Creator's direct voice. The Qumran community was deeply concerned with corpse impurity — the Miqtsat Ma'ase HaTorah (4QMMT) includes corpse impurity disputes among the key issues separating the community from the Jerusalem establishment. The three-day and seventh-day purification schedule for corpse impurity reflects the graduated purification system that runs throughout the Temple Scroll.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Bamidbar 19 — the red heifer legislation — in full. The Orit Ge'ez also preserves Vayikra 11 and 15 — the comprehensive purity legislation for objects and persons. The red heifer is theologically unique in the covenant purity system: the one who performs the purification (sprinkling the waters) becomes impure while the one who is sprinkled becomes pure. The Temple Scroll preserves this paradox in verse 12: the clean person who sprinkles shall be unclean until evening — the purifier bears the impurity of the purified.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Bamidbar 19:1-22 provides the complete red heifer legislation that Column 20 draws on. The Masoretic text is itself unusual in never explaining the theological rationale for the red heifer — it is simply commanded as a statute. The Temple Scroll follows the same pattern: presenting the red heifer as divine command without theological explanation, trusting the covenant community to accept Creator's authority without requiring covenant reasoning. The statute is sufficient because Creator has commanded it.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — any person who does not purify himself defiles the sanctuary of YHWH and shall be cut off from Yisra'EL. The dual consequence — sanctuary defilement and covenant excommunication — reflects the Temple Scroll's understanding of the severity of corpse impurity. Uncleanness is not a private matter: it threatens the entire covenant community's sanctuary access. This is why the Temple Scroll requires active purification rather than passive waiting: the unclean person has a covenant obligation to purify himself, not merely to stay away from the sanctuary while impure.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 21 Skin Disease — The Examination by the Priest — The Quarantine — The Purification of the Healed — The Houses of the City Examined for Plague — The Separation of the Afflicted

1 When a man has a swelling or an eruption or a spot on the skin of his body and it turns into a case of leprous disease on the skin of his body, then he shall be brought to Aharon the priest or to one of his sons the priests.

2 The priest shall examine the diseased area on the skin of his body — and if the hair in the diseased area has turned white and the disease appears to be deeper than the skin of his body it is a leprous disease.

3 The priest shall examine him and pronounce him unclean — he shall be pronounced unclean on account of the disease on his head.

4 The leprous person who has the disease shall wear torn clothes and let the hair of his head hang loose and cover his upper lip and shall cry out Unclean Unclean.

5 He shall remain unclean as long as he has the disease — he is unclean — he shall dwell alone and his dwelling shall be outside the camp.

6 And you shall make three places to the east of the city separated from one another — into these places shall come those who are smitten with a skin disease.

7 And they shall not enter the city of the sanctuary as long as they are afflicted — lest they defile the city with their uncleanness.

8 And any person smitten with discharge shall dwell outside the city of the sanctuary for seven days — and he shall come within on the eighth day when he is clean.

9 And any blind man shall not enter the city of the sanctuary all his days — he shall not defile the city in the midst of which I dwell.

10 When a plague of disease comes upon a house in the city of the sanctuary the priest shall come and examine the house — and if the plague has spread in the walls he shall order the house evacuated.

11 And the priest shall order that they take out of the house everything in it before the priest goes in to examine the plague — lest all that is in the house become unclean.

12 And after the house is examined if the plague has spread in the walls with greenish or reddish spots and appears deeper than the surface it is a persistent plague and the stones in which the plague is shall be removed.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 21 covers the skin disease (tsara'at) legislation and the segregation of the afflicted from the sanctuary city. The three designated places to the east of the city for the afflicted (verse 6) reflect the Qumran community's graduated exclusion system: the most severely afflicted are removed furthest from the sanctuary, while lesser degrees of impurity require shorter distances. The Community Rule's description of who may not enter the council of the community (blind, deaf, lame) mirrors the Temple Scroll's exclusion of these persons from the sanctuary city.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Vayikra 13-14 — the complete skin disease legislation — in full. The Orit Ge'ez Vayikra 14:33-53 preserves the house plague legislation that Column 21 verses 10-12 reflect. The Orit Ge'ez skin disease chapters are among the most detailed in the entire Vayikra legislation, reflecting the covenant community's intense concern with visible bodily afflictions as indicators of covenant purity status. The Temple Scroll applies this Vayikra legislation to the specific context of the sanctuary city.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Vayikra 13:1-46 provides the foundational skin disease legislation for Column 21. The Masoretic Bamidbar 5:2-3 — command the children of Yisra'EL to put out of the camp every leper and everyone having a discharge — provides the wilderness camp precedent that the Temple Scroll applies to the sanctuary city. The three designated eastern locations for the afflicted in verse 6 go beyond the Masoretic text's simple requirement of exclusion from the camp, adding the spatial specificity that characterises the Temple Scroll's approach to all purity legislation.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — any blind man shall not enter the city of the sanctuary all his days — he shall not defile the city in the midst of which I dwell. The permanent exclusion of blind persons from the sanctuary city is the Temple Scroll's most stringent application of the Levitical blemish legislation. The Rule of the Congregation (1QSa) applies the same exclusion to the eschatological assembly because of the presence of the angels. The Temple Scroll applies it to the ongoing covenant city for the same reason: Creator himself dwells in the midst of the city, and the same purity standards that apply to the angelic assembly apply to the divine dwelling.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 22 The Purification of the City — The Defilement of the Dead Removed — The Waters of Lustration Applied — The Cleansing of Metal Vessels and Garments — The City Made Holy Again

1 And when the dead man is removed from the house on the day of his death the house shall be sprinkled with the waters of lustration — all who are in the house shall be sprinkled.

2 And on the third day and on the seventh day you shall sprinkle those who have been defiled — and on the seventh day they shall wash their clothes and bathe and at sunset they shall be clean.

3 All the vessels of the house shall be dipped in water — vessels of wood and vessels of skin and vessels of bone — all shall be dipped.

4 But vessels of clay shall be broken — you shall not dip them and you shall not use them again — for they cannot be made clean from the impurity of the dead.

5 And every garment and every skin article and every object made of goat hair shall be washed in water and shall be unclean until evening and then shall be clean.

6 And all the silver and gold and bronze and iron and tin and lead — all metal vessels — shall be passed through fire and made clean.

7 But whatever cannot endure the fire shall be passed through water — it shall be immersed in water and shall be clean at sunset.

8 And the person who gathers the ashes of the red heifer shall wash his clothes and shall be unclean until evening — it is a statute forever to the children of Yisra'EL.

9 And the man who sprinkles the waters of lustration shall wash his clothes — and the one who touches the water of lustration shall be unclean until evening.

10 And whatever the unclean person touches shall be unclean — and the person who touches it shall be unclean until evening.

11 These are the statutes of purification from the impurity of the dead — a statute forever throughout your generations.

12 And thus shall you keep the city of the sanctuary holy before me — that my glory may rest in it forever and that my name may dwell there to all generations says YHWH.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 22 completes the corpse impurity and purification legislation cycle, drawing on Bamidbar 19 and Bamidbar 31. The graduated material purification requirements — clay vessels broken, metal passed through fire, organic materials washed — reflect the covenant community's understanding that different materials have different capacities for purification. Clay cannot be purified from corpse impurity and must be destroyed. Metal can be purified by fire. Organic materials can be purified by washing. The Temple Scroll's material hierarchy of purifiability is the most detailed in any ancient covenant legal text.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Bamidbar 31:19-24 — the purification of metal and organic materials after battle contact with the dead — which provides the base text for verses 5-7 of Column 22. The Orit Ge'ez Vayikra 11:32-35 provides the clay vessel legislation — clay vessels that become unclean must be broken (verse 4 of Column 22). The Temple Scroll integrates these separate Masoretic passages into a comprehensive purification protocol for all materials, applying the covenant principle that different materials require different purification methods.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Bamidbar 19:17-22 and 31:19-24 provide the foundational purification protocols for Column 22. The Masoretic distinction between materials that can endure fire (metal — passed through fire) and materials that cannot (organic — washed in water) is preserved exactly in Column 22 verse 6-7. The Temple Scroll adds the clay vessel destruction requirement from Vayikra 11 to complete the material purification taxonomy. The result is the most comprehensive covenant statement of post-corpse-impurity purification in any single document.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — thus shall you keep the city of the sanctuary holy before me — that my glory may rest in it forever and that my name may dwell there to all generations. The closing declaration of Column 22 connects the practical purity legislation of the preceding columns to their theological purpose: every regulation about corpse impurity, skin disease, latrines, and material purification serves the single goal of keeping the sanctuary city pure enough for Creator's glory to rest in it permanently. The elaborate purity system is not an end in itself — it is the covenant infrastructure that maintains the divine presence.
